The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango (28): Browsing Your Atlas Database with MongoDB Compass
In the last chapter we went through signing up for a remote MongoDB database. But how do we actually use it? That's what this chapter is for.
So how do we connect to the remote database we just signed up for? Honestly the easiest way is to use a GUI tool, and the one I'd recommend here is the official one:
I won't walk through downloading and installing it, so let's jump straight into the MongoDB Compass interface.
A wall of English text scares plenty of people off, so here's a translated version of the screen.
Now let's grab the connection string for the MongoDB Atlas database we signed up for. If you can't find it, head back to this page.
There's a Connect button up top. Click it and it'll ask which method you want to use to connect to MongoDB.
The one we want here is MongoDB Compass, so pick "Connect using MongoDB Compass".
Once you click that, you'll land on two options:
I do not have MongoDB Compass
We're obviously going with "I have MongoDB Compass" here — unless you skipped installing it earlier.

After clicking "I have MongoDB Compass", you'll see two bits of info. The first is your MongoDB Compass version, but since we just downloaded it, you can leave that setting alone. The second is the connection URL — go ahead and copy it.
Once you've copied it, you'll notice something a little odd in there —
That password is the one for the user account you set up earlier. So to be accurate, it should really look like this:
Then paste that connection string into MongoDB Compass.
After filling it in, hit the Connect button. It takes a moment, so give it a sec.
If you hit an error after connecting — something like
— check whether one of these is the culprit:
Did you add your current machine's IP to the allowlist?
Did you fat-finger the username or password?
Once you're connected successfully, the screen will look like this.
Congrats — you're connected to your remote MongoDB database. By default there are two databases here, and as usual I've attached a translated screenshot.
. It comes with a bunch of default collections you can play around with.
So that's a basic intro to MongoDB Compass. Most of the time we'll be reaching for Compass when we just want to eyeball our data.

In the last chapter we set up a remote MongoDB database over on Atlas. But now what? How do we actually use the thing? This chapter walks through connecting to your remote database with MongoDB Compass, the official GUI tool — copying the connection string, swapping in your password, and poking around your collections.
